title: RBAC IaC – Azure Access Management as Code
desc: Replaced ad-hoc, portal-driven Azure access management with a fully declarative, GitOps-style RBAC pipeline. Every identity, role assignment, and permission boundary across three environments is defined in YAML, reviewed in pull requests, and reconciled automatically, including zero-trust ABAC constraints and time-boxed PIM eligibility for privileged roles.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: Each environment lives on its own Git branch with its own YAML config fragments, merged by a small Python script into a single manifest before every deployment. A Bash-based deploy flow drives that manifest against Azure through GitHub Actions' OIDC federation (no stored credentials), creating or updating managed identities, RBAC role assignments, Cosmos DB data-plane roles, and Azure SQL database roles (the latter via Entra-authenticated sqlcmd calls that idempotently create database users and add them to roles).

Privileged assignments can be marked PIM-eligible with a configurable expiry instead of granted permanently, and ABAC condition strings can be attached directly to role assignments to restrict exactly which roles an identity is allowed to grant, preventing privilege escalation even for administrative identities.

On every run, the pipeline compares the manifest against live Azure state and removes anything no longer defined in YAML, so configuration drift never accumulates; user-facing role assignments are the one exception, kept additive-only to avoid accidentally locking people out.

title: On-Prem Virtualization Platform as Code
desc: Designed and built a self-hosted, on-prem infrastructure platform from the ground up: one push to production provisions VMs with Terraform and configures them with Ansible, covering DNS, internal PKI, SIEM, EDR, and application hosting, with zero manual steps. On top of it: a production RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) platform with SSO, and an ERP host with automated backups.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: The provisioning layer uses the bpg/proxmox Terraform provider to clone new VMs from a cloud-init template, wiring up CPU, memory, disk, and networking, with Terraform state kept remotely in Azure Storage and authenticated via OIDC rather than static keys. A small Python bridge script converts Terraform's outputs into a generated Ansible inventory, so the configuration stage never needs to know about infrastructure state directly. A chain of GitHub Actions workflows takes over from there: a Terraform apply triggers an Ansible run only once it succeeds.

Every VM in the fleet gets the same baseline through shared Ansible roles regardless of its job: SSH key distribution, common hardening, an EDR agent, a SIEM agent enrolled against the central manager, and a leaf certificate from an internal CA so it can serve TLS the rest of the fleet trusts.

On top of that shared baseline, each VM carries one specialised role: a self-managed ERP instance on PostgreSQL with scheduled database and filestore backups pushed to Azure Blob Storage; a single-node SIEM manager that every other VM ships logs and audit events to, with a dedicated SSD-backed data disk for the indexer's growing storage; a minimal internal certificate authority issuing the leaf certificates the rest of the fleet trusts, kept deliberately tiny since a CA has almost no footprint; a self-hosted GitHub Actions runner that executes the deploy jobs from inside the network so the hypervisor's management API never needs to be exposed to the internet; the RAG platform's data plane, running LightRAG together with PostgreSQL extended with pgvector and AGE for the vector store and knowledge graph, calling out to a separate GPU host for inference rather than running any heavy model locally; the RAG platform's front door, running Caddy terminating TLS issued by the internal CA, oauth2-proxy enforcing Microsoft Entra single sign-on and group membership, and a FastAPI backend holding the API key server-side; and an internal DNS server, authoritative for internal zones.
A smaller sandbox VM rounds out the fleet, used to validate changes to the Terraform and Ansible roles themselves before they touch anything production.

title: Local LLM Inference Host
desc: Turned a bare NVIDIA DGX Spark box into a hardened, self-hosted LLM inference platform: several local models served behind an authenticated reverse proxy, speculative decoding for faster responses, and full audit and SIEM visibility into every request. Now the model backend behind an internal RAG platform.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: The core of the setup is an Ansible role that builds llama.cpp from source with CUDA support on the DGX Spark's ARM64 hardware, pinned to a specific upstream commit to guarantee Multi-Token Prediction support, then runs it as a systemd-managed router service exposing several model profiles (including a large MoE model tuned with speculative decoding for lower latency) behind a single API.

Alongside it, the role deploys and hardens a diffusion model server, OpenWebUI as a chat front-end, and ComfyUI for image and video generation, each as its own systemd unit with its own service user and log files. All of these sit behind a single Caddy reverse proxy that terminates TLS and enforces bearer-token API-key authentication before any request reaches a model.

To make the host observable rather than a black box, the role also configures structured JSON access logging, persistent journald storage, log rotation, and auditd rules that watch configuration files, secrets, and privileged service commands; it then enrolls the host as a SIEM agent so all of that telemetry flows into the same central monitoring used across the rest of the infrastructure.

title: Azure IaC Program – Migrating a Cloud Estate to Code
desc: Beyond individual platforms, a broader effort ported nearly an entire company's Azure estate from manual, portal-driven changes into version-controlled, environment-aware Infrastructure as Code, one domain at a time. Each area got its own Bicep templates and its own GitHub Actions pipeline authenticated via OIDC, following a consistent dev/beta/prod branching pattern.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: Networking – core VNets for dev, beta, and prod, each with its own non-overlapping address space, subnets for database, web, storage, key vault, function app, and API management workloads, private DNS zones, and DMZ peering, all provisioned from a single parameterised Bicep template.

DMZ – the public edge of the network: a dedicated DMZ VNet peered to the intranet, and an Application Gateway (WAF_v2) with its listeners, backend pools, probes, routing rules, redirects, and rewrites.

WAF rules – the Web Application Firewall policy attached to that gateway: custom rule sets, log scrubbing for sensitive fields such as passwords, environment-aware switching between detection and prevention mode, and a temporary, time-boxed pentest IP allowlist for non-production.

Storage estate – a primary and geo-replicated SQL Server pair behind a manual-failover group, two Cosmos DB accounts, and their supporting storage account, checked against the live environment with az deployment group what-if until the plan showed zero creates or deletes: a faithful, drift-free match for what was already running in production.
Static frontend hosting – Storage-Account-backed static website hosting behind private endpoints, with public blob access and shared-key authentication disabled and RBAC enforced.

AKS platform – an Azure Kubernetes Service cluster and Container Registry in Bicep, with a GitOps-style pipeline that follows infrastructure provisioning with a Helm-based rollout of Envoy Gateway and cert-manager for automated Let's Encrypt certificates.

Security tooling – Microsoft Defender for Cloud and Defender for Storage with environment-appropriate coverage, Microsoft Sentinel, subscription and resource diagnostic settings, and an automated Function App that renews Let's Encrypt certificates in Key Vault in response to expiry notifications.
Access to every resource provisioned across this estate is, in turn, governed by the RBAC pipeline above, closing the loop between what gets deployed and who is allowed to touch it.

title: SQLmap DAST – Automated SQL Injection Testing for Production APIs
desc: Built a scheduled dynamic application security testing (DAST) pipeline that runs SQLMap against the company's own beta APIs every week, without anyone having to hand-craft a single request. Findings, evidence, and raw scan output are packaged into a report on every run, so injection risks in new endpoints get caught automatically instead of waiting on a manual pentest cycle.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: Each scan starts from the same OpenAPI schemas the API team already maintains: a GitHub Actions workflow authenticates as a GitHub App to pull the latest processed schema from a sister repository, then a Python request builder walks every path and method in that schema and synthesises a realistic HTTP request for each one, choosing sensible values by parameter name and type and, where possible, pulling real values back from the live API first so the generated request bodies look like genuine traffic rather than placeholder data.

Each request carries a short-lived bearer token obtained by logging in as a dedicated test account, plus a scanner-specific header that lets it through the WAF rules defined in a companion IaC repository. SQLMap then runs against every prepared request in batch mode with per-request timeouts, and its raw output is parsed to pull out vulnerable parameters, the injection technique, and the detected backend DBMS.

Results are compiled into a summary table, a full Markdown report, and a rendered PDF, alongside a machine-readable JSON result, all published as workflow artifacts with secrets and auth tokens stripped out before anything is stored. A dedicated cleanup job runs immediately afterwards to remove any test data the scan created, so the automation leaves no trace behind.

title: OWASP ZAP DAST – Broad Vulnerability Scanning Wired into GitHub Code Scanning
desc: Built a second, complementary DAST pipeline that runs OWASP ZAP's active and passive scanners against every major beta API surface (portal, admin portal, vendor portal, mobile, public, access controller, and provisioning), on demand from GitHub Actions, with results flowing straight into GitHub's code scanning tab rather than sitting in a report nobody opens.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: Seven near-identical per-API workflows were consolidated into a single reusable workflow that each caller invokes with its own automation plan and report names. Every run authenticates as a GitHub App to fetch the latest OpenAPI schema, then obtains a bearer token from the API's own /auth endpoint, failing the run immediately, before any unauthenticated scanning happens, if a token can't be obtained, and masking that token so it never appears in logs or step outputs.

ZAP then runs the matching automation plan inside the official zaproxy/zap-stable container, with custom HTTP-sender scripts injecting the bearer token and a WAF-bypass header on every in-scope request, and a dedicated safety script rewriting the IDs on any state-changing request during the active scan so it can never modify or delete a real tester's data. Scan duration, rule timeouts, and thread count are tuned per plan to keep runs bounded.

Because ZAP's native JSON report doesn't map cleanly onto GitHub's SARIF format, a small custom GitHub Action was built to convert ZAP's findings into SARIF, which is what lets every scan's results show up as annotated alerts in the repository's Security tab instead of a report artifact that has to be opened by hand. A cleanup job removes any test data the scan created immediately afterwards, mirroring the same safety net used on the SQLmap side.

title: External Penetration Testing Coordination
desc: Acted as the internal owner of third-party penetration testing engagements with external security firms, driving each one end to end rather than handing it off after a kickoff call, from initial scoping through to the final retest.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: Defined test requirements and scope together with each vendor up front, translating what the business actually needed tested into a scope the testers could execute against, and prepared dedicated test environments, accounts, sample data, and network access, so testing could run safely without touching real customer data or disrupting production traffic.

Once a report came back, findings were reviewed in detail and triaged by real risk rather than taken at face value, then routed to the right engineering owners with enough context to act on them, not just a CVSS score and a screenshot.

After remediation, each engagement was closed out with a retest against the original findings to confirm the fixes actually held, and recurring patterns across engagements fed back into the company's baseline security posture, including WAF rules, RBAC configuration, and DAST tooling, rather than being treated as one-off findings.

title: ISO 27001-Aligned Security Policy Program
desc: Designed and stood up the company's information security policy framework essentially from a blank page, with no formal ISMS or documented security policies in place beforehand, aligned to ISO 27001's control domains.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: Authored a full set of policies covering information security governance, access control, asset management, security awareness and training, backup and recovery, third-party and supplier risk, logging and monitoring, DevSecOps, vulnerability management, cloud security, and data protection and retention, then took ownership of driving each one from an initial draft through stakeholder review to an actual, tracked rollout rather than letting them sit as unused documents.

Each policy is tracked against a concrete implementation checklist rather than treated as finished once it's signed off. Several, including asset management and security awareness training, are already fully embedded into day-to-day operations, and the remainder are in active, staged rollout against a defined roadmap, with progress reviewed on a recurring basis.

Where a policy created a concrete technical requirement, implementation fed directly into the tooling built elsewhere: access control policy into the RBAC IaC pipeline, logging and monitoring policy into the SIEM and EDR rollout across the on-prem fleet, and vulnerability management policy into the SQLmap and ZAP DAST pipelines, so policy and engineering stayed in sync rather than diverging.

title: EU Cyber Resilience Act (CRA) Compliance Program
desc: Built the company's Cyber Resilience Act compliance program from the ground up, translating a brand-new EU regulation into a concrete, owned roadmap for an IoT product line spanning a web platform, mobile app, physical sensors, and gateways, including OEM-branded hardware. No CRA compliance work existed before this, and the regulation itself was new enough that there was no established internal playbook to build from.

detail.label: technical breakdown
paragraphs: The program is structured around the CRA's two real deadlines: a near-term Article 14 reporting obligation that becomes applicable in 2026, and full product conformity against Annex I, II, and VII by the final 2027 deadline.

Working from the regulation text and official EU guidance, broke the requirement down into a tracked checklist of roughly fifty individual controls, spanning vulnerability reporting and coordinated disclosure, SBOM and dependency management, incident response, secure-by-design and secure-by-default engineering practices, access control, data protection, logging, secure update delivery, supply chain and build-pipeline security, conformity assessment and CE marking, and the specific supplier-assurance obligations that apply to OEM-branded hardware.

On top of the checklist, consolidated the resulting documentation requirement into ten Technical File packages aligned to CRA Annex VII, each with a defined owner, evidence location, and priority, so audit-readiness work has a single structure to point to instead of being scattered across teams. Some of the underlying controls, like dependency and vulnerability scanning across the estate, were already in place from prior security engineering work and slotted directly into the new framework rather than needing to be rebuilt.

title: ConsultIT – Security Assessment Report
desc: Laureate project for the ConsultIT competition (SGH, with Accenture, LOT, P&G, Allegro, GFT). Advanced through a CTF qualifier, then analyzed a real company's systems, processes, and architecture in the final, proposing concrete security improvements.
_note: Write-up lives in a private repo (github.com/JakubMlocek/CTF). Make it public and add a "repo" field above to link it.

title: HackAGH 2023 – Autonomous Vehicle App
desc: Finalist at HackAGH, a team hackathon organized by URSS AGH on autonomous-vehicle automotive tech. Devised a creative autonomous-car feature and built a mobile app prototype, presented live to a jury in the final.
